image_caption: Jayabali Dyaḥ, view from E
monument_description: Jayabali Dyaḥ is a pīṭha (abode) of Goddess Rudrāyaṇī, one of the Eight Mother Goddesses ( aṣṭamātṛkā ) whose shrines date back to the foundation of Sankhu in in 1300 BC (Kaligata Era 1801). monument_description: Situated next to the steps that lead towards the Vajrayoginī Mandira ( SKH0500 ), Siṃhapū Sataḥ opens up to the east, with five wood-carved bays on the front side and three bays facing south. monument_description: Built in 1706 CE (NS 826), this temple of Nāsaḥdyaḥ, the deity of music and charm, also has a waterhole revered as Bālakumārī. image_caption: Tāpālāchi Phalcā, view from SE
monument_description: Tāpālāchi Phalcā was built around the middle of the Rana period. Since then, the descendants of its builder have continued the tradition of distributing a ritual drink made of water and sugar ( sākhaḥti) that is distributed on the day of Akṣaya Tṛtīyā . This arcaded platform is part of several temples and two phalcās at Dhālāchẽ ṭola, in the open courtyard called Tāpalāchi, which is what gives this monument its name. Positioned east of the Bhaṣmeśvara Ghāṭa in the Pashupati area, the Viṣṇu Mandira is commonly known as the Rāmacandra Mandira despite housing a statue of Viṣṇu within its sanctum. monument_description: The Mātakhyaḥ Śikharakūṭa Caitya is established at the rectangular courtyard of the Mātakhyaḥ Ṭola in the eastern border of Kirtipur. image_caption: Umāmaheśvara Mandira, view from NW
monument_description: Umāmaheśvara Temple (made of bricks) is constructed in the śikhara– style and faces west. The finial of the Temple made in a kalaśa -shape is gilded. Additionally, a statue of Sūrya (in the west), Gaṇeśa (in the north) and Viṣṇu (in the east) and Gaṇeśa (in the south) have been installed on the double-roofed pavilion above the sanctum. There are no historical records to date the initial construction of the Temple

image_caption: Nārāyaṇa Mandira, view from SW
monument_description: The Nārāyaṇa Mandira stands south of the road from Mangal Bazaar to Sundhārā. With a one-tier roof of jhĩga ṭ ī tiles, the temple faces west. A metal finial tops the roof with three patā ḥ s made of brass hanging down from the finial to the eaves of the roof. It is located next to the Bhīmasena Mandira (known as Nugaḥ Bhindyaḥ or Puṃ Bhindyaḥ) in Sundhara. It faces east and has three stories. Certain Śreṣṭhas having family name Mulmī built this Bāhāḥ in NS 835 (1715 CE) and appointed a Vajrācārya family living in the area to maintain and run it. image_caption: Indrāyaṇī Pīṭha, view from NW
monument_description: The Indrāyaṇī pīṭha represents one of the eight Mātṛkās ( Aṣṭamātṛkā ) that traditionally encircle the city of Patan. According to long-standing custom, the residents of Patan visit this shrine during the month of Yaṃlā, just before the Daśaĩ (Daśain) festival, proceeding in musical procession ( bājāgājā ) to perform ritual worship at midday (local resident, Mahārjan, male, age 63). The concept of the Aṣṭamātṛkā encircling a city is deeply rooted in the sacred geography of the Kathmandu Valley.

image_caption: Dāsa Dhārā, view from W
monument_description: The Dāsa Dhārā (water spout) is situated along the traditional route linking Triśūlī Bazaar to Nuwakot, a passage widely used by people before the construction of a motor road in the area. The spout's primary purpose was to provide drinking water for travellers passing by. image_caption: Neuta Phalcā, view from SE
monument_description: This phalcā is located at Neuta Ṭola and faces towards the east. It is said to have been built by a Jośī clan from Bakhuṃ Bāhāḥ, though no documented evidence has so far been found with which one might ascertain the date of its initial construction. The phalcā had one storey before it collapsed during the earthquake of VS 1990 (1934 CE). After this, it began to be used as a platform ( ḍabalī or dabū ). The phalcā was rebuilt in 2006 CE (VS 2069) under the supervision of the Khapiṃchẽ Youth Club

monument_description: Despite its resemblance to a sataḥ (rest-house), this structure serves as a temple housing a statue of Lord Rāma, earning it the designation of Rāma Mandira. Situated along the banks of the Bagmati River in the Hanumān Ghāṭa area, a full-sized statue of Hanumān stands guard in front. monument_description: Located in Māthillo Haṭiyā, Dāpcā, the temple stands unusually in the middle of a road, permitting traffic to pass on both sides. image_caption: Vasu Bāhāḥ, kvāḥpāḥdegaḥ , view from NE
monument_description: Vasu Bāhāḥ is a branch of Rudravarṇa Mahāvihāra, Oku Bāhāḥ ( LAL2260 ). Unlike other bāhāḥs in Patan and elsewhere, this Bāhāḥ does not have a proper shrine ( kvāḥpāḥchẽ ) for the Bāhāḥ’s primary deity ( kvāpāḥdyaḥ ). Instead of a kvāḥpāḥchẽ , a small temple is built in the Bāhāḥ’s courtyard (called kvāḥpāḥdegaḥ ), and a statue of Akṣobhya is enshrined inside its sanctum. image_caption: Bāḍegāũ Hiti, view from W
monument_description: Initially built to provide water for the daily worship of Ṣaḍakṣarī Lokeśvara, this hiti was renovated by Rāṇā Prime Minister Candra Śamśera in 1912 CE (VS 1969). Located south of the Bāḍegāũ Caitya ( BDG0040 ) in Bāḍegāũ, this water spout has been named the Bāḍegāũ Hiti for this documentation, as it does not have any name in particular. monument_description: Micchu Bāhāḥ (built before 1661 CE) is a branch of Kvā Bāhāḥ (LAL1110). It has a saṅgha consisting of Śākya families. The barechuyegu initiation for its saṅgha members is performed in front of the caitya , but not in front of the shrine of the kvāḥpāḥdyaḥ . As such, they are known as cibhāḥ bare or cailaka bandeju . monument_description: Nṛtyeśvara Temple is located in the Khāchẽ courtyard of Khāchẽ Ṭola just south of Nāga Bāhāḥ (LAL0930). Four passageways towards the north, west, south and southeast connect the Khāchẽ courtyard with the main road and other courtyards. monument_description: Nestled within the palace square, the Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa Temple features two tiers. Similar smaller temples adorn the four corners of the Taleju temple (KAT1570), as well as both sides (KAT1550, KAT1560) of its main entrance. Facing west, the Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa temple showcases intricate wood carvings on its doors and windows, with corner struts bearing carved winged horses ( kuṃsala ). monument_description: Of Jaṅga Bahādura's many queens, the one from Gorakhpur constructed this temple and donated a vast tract of land as a religious endowment. monument_description: Bhaulvāḥ Gaṇeśa Temple, located in the Bholāchẽ Ṭola, not only houses Gaṇeśa within its sanctum, but also a Bhagavatī statue. There was a tradition that even Taleju, the tutelary deity of the Malla kings, worshipped her. The terracotta Temple is built in the śikharakūṭa -style and faces south. image_caption: Nugaḥ Phalcā 3, view from W (May 2019)
monument_description: Nugaḥ Phalcā faces east and is located on the road from Nugaḥ Ṭola to Oku Bāhāḥ. Several other phalcās and temples can be found in its vicinity. This three-bayed Phalcā has a pointed roof and is engaged to a building in the south. The structure is a rare type of a mandaḥ phalcā with 16 columns. According to a member of the Ṭola Sudhāra Samiti, this latticework was installed to prevent street children and 'strangers' from using it. These days the Samiti uses the Phalcā as a storeroom. image_caption: Thyākā Bāhāḥ, Akṣobhya Buddha, view from E (July 2019)
monument_description: Thyākā Bāhāḥ is located near Naka Bahī ( LAL0880 ) to the right-hand side of the street on the way to Kvālkhu. The small courtyard of the Bāhāḥ measures 3.6 m x 3.8 m and is surrounded by residential buildings. The way to access the Bāhāḥ is not immediately obvious; one needs to pass through a shop to reach it. The courtyard is encircled by a stone plinth and a cement plastered floor. The main temple is a single storey structure built on a raised plinth that is 80 cm high. It is open to the east

monument_description: Bhīmasena Mandira is located at Bhindyaḥtvāḥ, Kathmandu. It faces east and has three roofs. Usually, the sanctum ( garbhagṛha ) of a temple is built on the ground floor. image_caption: Pīra Pāṭī, view from S
monument_description: Pīra Pāṭī is situated to the northwest of the Taleju temple, below an ancient and sacred pipal tree in Kathmandu Durbar Square. Beneath this tree is a small shrine that houses the revered impression of feet (pādukā), believed to belong to Gorakhanātha, a renowned guru of the Nātha tradition of asceticism. monument_description: Jena Bāhāḥ, also known as Śrī Jñāna Nāma Vihāra, is located on the southern side of Jena Bāhāḥ Ṭola in front of the Gaṇeśa Mandira ( LAL0270 ). The courtyard can be accessed by passing through a metal door. The main structure of the bāhāḥ has mostly collapsed. Its foundations as well as wooden decorative elements are scattered around the site. The main structure on the left side of the metal door has been destroyed and is fenced off with CGI sheets. The square courtyard has a teliya tiled floor

image_caption: Uttaragaṅgā Pillar, view from west
monument_description: A stone memorial pillar with the representation of a horse from circa 13th-15th century The pillar is located east of the village of Uttaragaṅgā, situated south-west of Birendranagar. The artefact is installed near the entrance to the small and recent Jaleśvara Mandira. The upper register of the object contain the carved representation of a horse on the eastern and western sides. The western side is the most eroded. Horse riders are commonly found represented on contemporary stone memorial pilla

image_caption: Māju Degaḥ, view from E
monument_description: The Mājū Degaḥ is situated within the Kathmandu Durbar Square precinct, immediately east of the Jośī Āgaṃcheṃ ( KAT1900 ). Among the temples of the Hanumānaḍhokā palace complex, it is regarded as the tallest and most imposing structure after the Taleju and Degutale temples. In Nepalabhāṣā, the word māju means “mother”; since the temple was commissioned by Ṛddhilakṣmī, the mother of King Bhūpālendra Malla, it came to be known as Mājū Degah (also Mājudevala). The Sataḥ partly collapsed in the 2015 earthquakes and its superstructure was dismantled shortly thereafter. Today only remnants of the foundation plinth and a few carved base stones representing lions remain on the overgrown site. image_caption: Iḍola Phalcā, view from E
monument_description: Iḍola Phalcā, named after its location for this documentation as its specific name is unknown, is a square, two-bayed Phalcā in the Iḍola fields of northeastern Bhaktapur. It differs from the Valley's typical rectangular single-bayed shelters. Its gable roof is supported by two central pillars with uniquely carved capitals ( metha ), possibly older than other wooden components. Traditionally, these public shelters, found not only along roadsides but also within farmlands, provided farmers refuge

image_caption: Santabhogeśvara, western façade, view from W

image_caption: Ubāhāḥ Bahī, main entrance door, view from W
monument_description: Ubāhāḥ Bahī is located to the east of Oku Bāhāḥ ( LAL2260 ). The main entrance of the Bahī faces west, and can be found on the route to Sic̃āhitī when one turns right. As the only original element on the façade, it is easily identifiable. The lintels of the main door frame are shared by the the two buildings to the left and right of the monument. Tyāṅgā Bhairava is also known locally as Tyāṅkā Bhairava and Tīkā Bhairava. During the Nepal–Tibet/China War (1791–1792 CE), the Chinese forces advanced toward Nepal but were successfully driven back from Jītapuraphedī. Even today (as of August 2025), water flows year-round from the ancient hiti (stone water spout) located here in this historic Jītpurphedī. In all four directions of Kathmandu, there are four gateways ( Bhaṅjyaṅga ). image_caption: Karuṇāmaya Phalcā, view from S
monument_description: The Phalcā gets its name from the statue of Karuṇāmaya, a popular name of Padmapāṇi Lokeśvara, enshrined inside the Phalcā as the central deity. Two other statues–Vajrasattva to his right and Tārā to his left–are also enshrined there. Originally, the Phalcā had three bays and traditional look, but it was reconstructed in ca. 1998 CE into a concrete structure with a cover of a cement slab on top. The nhāykaṃlvahaṃ s and dāciaḥpā s seen on the base of the Phalcā belonged to its previous structure.

image_caption: Vādey Jaḥdhuṃ, view from E
monument_description: Built in 1657 CE (NS 777) by three brothers: Kāśī Bhāro, Gaṃgārāma Bhāro, and Śiva Bhāro, this jaḥdhuṃ water tank is now in a state of disrepair and is not in use. Located on the road from Cāpāgāũ to Bulu in Pāulī Ṭola, this artistic water tank that requires manual filling of water is crowned with three stone ceremonial mirrors ( jvalānhāykaṃ ) as its finials. A statue of Bhagīratha can be seen below the breast-shaped spout. Such jaḥdhuṃ tanks were built by the roadside in the Kathmandu valley

image_caption: Thāymaru Vāsyāḥdyaḥ Phalcā, view from E
monument_description: Thāymaru Vāsyāḥdyaḥ Phalcā is ritually and physically connected to the shrine of Vāsyāḥdyaḥ (lit., deity of toothache), a large unhewn piece of wood believed to have the power to heal dental pain if worshipped. A portion of the wood revered as Vāsyāḥdyaḥ reaches into the Phalcā, across the floor of the three-bayed site. There used to be a water tank ( jaḥdhuṃ ) below the unhewn wood. The tank was previously filled from an opening in the Phalcā. The water tank is now hidden by the wall built belo

monument_description: Constructed in 1664 CE (NS 784) by a group led by Bhavānīśaṅkara of Kirtipur, the main statue of this temple was established by Bhavānīdāsa. A procession of Kālikā and Bhairava is taken out every year on the 1st of the bright fortnight of Caitra. Standing in the middle of the Kisipiḍi settlement, also known as Kipulcā, this two-story temple has a tiled roof with a balconied tower ( phucā ) on top. The tower has copper sheets laid on the roof, crowned with a metallic finial bearing an oblong bann

image_caption: Nārāyaṇa Mandira, view from W
monument_description: Situated in an alley of the upper old market in Triśulī, this temple was constructed in the Śivālaya architectural style, commonly seen in Kathmandu and its surrounding areas during the Rāṇā period. The temple, though modest in size, is crafted with stone floors, traditional brickwork, and intricately carved wooden doors. The temple’s entrance features carvings from the Rāṇā and Śāha periods, including a depiction of Nārāyaṇa. Flanking the door are two wall niches housing stone statues of Bhīmas

image_caption: Tārā Nani, view from NE
monument_description: Tārā Nani is one of the branches of Ituṃ Bāhāḥ ( KAT1220 ) and is located to its north. None of the bāhā ḥ' s architectural features are preserved in the Nani. A two-storey temple of Tārā is built in the middle of the Nani courtyard and thus, it is called Tārā Nani. The roofs of the temple are covered with copper plates and the wooden tora ṇ a above the entrance of the sanctum is wrapped in bronze. An image of Tārā showing the dharmacakra gesture ( mudrā ) is carved in the middle of the tora

image_caption: Jaladyaḥ Sataḥ, front facade, view from S
monument_description: This is one of the rare arcaded rest places where two of the most important ritual dance groups, even deities, come to perform: The Navadurgās, dancing deities from Bhaktapur and mask dancers from the city of Harisiddhi use the space of this arcaded rest house ( sataḥ ) while they come to the Paśupati area to perform. Built on the north-western side of the Jayavāgīśvarī Mandira ( PPK1222 ), the Jaladyaḥ Sataḥ also stands opposite another arcaded rest house known as the Triśūla Sataḥ ( PPK1216 ).

image_caption: Kuladīpā Bāhāḥ, view from E
monument_description: Kuladīpā Bāhāḥ is located near the confluence of the Bāgmatī and Viṣṇumatī (Keśavatī) rivers. Newar Buddhists worship this confluence as the Cintāmaṇi Tirtha and therefore the bāhāḥ's Sanskrit name is Cintāmaṇi Mahāvihāra. Newar Buddhists believe that Varuṇa Nāgarāja resides at the confluence and worshipping there makes their dreams come true ( Śākya & Vajrācārya 1121, 117). Kuladīpā Bāhāḥ was built by a certain Kuladīpa Vajrācārya in 1941 CE (NS 1061) and is an idenpendent bāhāḥ (not a branch).

image_caption: Bhindyaḥlāchi Phalcā, damaged southern wall, view from S
monument_description: This three-bay phalcā was donated by a family of the Avāle caste that lives across the lane. The phalcā is situated on the street which leads from Balkhu (south) to Cyāsal (north). It is adjacent to the house of Vinaya Avāle's on the western side and to the house of Āśiṣa Avāle on the south. When the house on the south was demolished the phalcā lost its wall. Five columns and capitals dating from ca. 1880 have been preserved. The guṭhī is from the caste of Avāle.

image_caption: Lakṣmībhakteśvara Mandira, view from S
monument_description: The Lakṣmībhakteśvara temple stands as a tribute to Lakṣmīdāsa Pradhāna, a pivotal mediator of governmental information for over five decades from the early 19th century. Lakṣmīdāsa held the important post of mīra munsī , the head of the munsī s, within the Munsīkhānā, an institution responsible for managing foreign affairs and correspondence. This position was significant during the governance of Bhīmasena Thāpā from 1806 to 1837 CE, and it continued until the fall of the Rāṇā regime in 1951 CE

image_caption: Vatsalā Mandira, view from W
monument_description: Goddess Vatsalā, also known as Vatsaleśvarī, is described as a form of Goddess Pārvatī in the Nepālamahātmya (1.39). As Lord Śiva had decided not to go to the Kailāśadhāma, his home, but remain in the Nepāla Valley, Pārvatī also decided to remain here with him. Since she wanted to stay in the Valley because of the love ( vātsalya ) for him, she came to be known as Vatsalā from then onwards ( Acharya 1992: p. 19). image_caption: Bhurtī Dhārā, right water spout, view from west
monument_description: A stone fountain from approximately 13th-15th century. The monument is found in the northern part of the Bhurtī Temple Complex. The site itself is located in the northern end of the settlement of Bhurtī, 3 km north of Narayana, the district headquarters. It currently comprises 22 standing śikhara temples (or devala ) and one stone fountain. Architectural activity at the site can be situated between the 13 th and 15 th century, a time period corresponding to the latter part of the Khaśa Malla emp

image_caption: Hitipine Phalcā, view from W
monument_description: The Hitipine Phalcā stands next to the road main used by pilgrims and devotees visiting the Paśupati Temple en route from the East, passing through cities like Thimi, Bhaktapur, Banepā, Panautī, etc. A person named Bhaṭṭa facilitated the construction of the Phalcā and the associated hiti to allow bypassers to rest and quench their thirst. According to the inscription, the monuments were once constructed in front of a water hole. The water from the water hole was used as the source for the hiti .

image_caption: Pakanājola Kṛṣṇa Mandira, view from NE
monument_description: Situated at Pakanājola in Kathmandu, this Temple of Lord Kṛṣṇa was constructed by a Pā̃ḍe family in the mid-19 th century on their private courtyard (see the inscription from VS 1909/ŚS1777 [ KAT0040_I_001 ], erected east of the Bhagavatī Temple [ KAT0050 ) on the same courtyard built by the same family). With only one entrance on the southern side, the Kṛṣṇa Temple is built on two plinths. The sanctum of the Temple houses a stone statue of Lord Kṛṣṇa flanked by his consorts Rukmiṇī and Satyabhā

image_caption: Dhaḥmā Phalcā, view from S
monument_description: The 13-bay phalcā is referred to as Dhaḥmā Phalcā because it houses the dhaḥmās , which serve as the longitudinal main beam of the Matsyendranāth chariot. A dhaḥmā (also sometimes referred to as ghaḥmā) is the shaft which is upturned at one end and passes through a chariot between the wheels. There is always a painting of Bhairava on the face of the upturned part. This is covered by a gilt copper image of the same deity. The d haḥmā itself is considered to be Karkoṭaka Nāgarāja, the serpent deit

monument_description: This is the āgama house of Valānimha group Rājopādhyāyas of Lalitpur. The courtyard can be accessed by entering through the underpass of a residential building. The main āgama building is completely ruined and only the wall of the principal east façade remains up to the height of the first floor. The first floor has a central threefold pasukājhyāḥ flanked by an aedicule to the south and a tikijhyāḥ to the north. The main entry door on the ground floor is decorated with images of Mahādeva and Pār

monument_description: Pūcva Cvaya Bahī is located at the highest point of Patan city to the west the Aśoka Stūpa at Pulchok. Pulchok's old name is Puraco derived from the combination of the Sanskrit word ‘ pura ('city') and Newari word co ('small hill'). The bahī , located on top of a small hill is called Puraco or Pulaco Bahī. This bahī is also called Pūcva Cvaya Bahī or Akṣeśvara Mahāvihāra. monument_name: Gaṇeśa Mandira (reconstructed in 2000 CE in its present look)
image_caption: Gaṇeśa Mandira, view from S
monument_description: Gaṇeśa Mandira is located at the Sāymi Ṭola, Kirtipur. This place is mostly inhabited by the members of the Mānandhara caste. A family from this caste runs a gu ṭ hī associated with the temple, sponsorsing for the display of a metal statue of Gaṇeśa in the temple every year on the full moon day of Śrāvaṇa (also called Guṃlāpunhī), on the Kṛṣṇāṣṭamī and Indra Jātrā Day. The gu ṭhī has no regular income in the present-day. The residents of Ṭola worship this Gaṇeśa before they start any auspicious

image_caption: Kavīndrapura Mandira, view from W
monument_description: Kavīndrapura is probably the only temple of a grand size dedicated to Nāsaḥdyaḥ, the deity of music and drama who is invariably represented only by a small triangular hole on a wall. Constructed in 1657 CE (NS 777) by King Pratāpa Malla, it used to be one of the tallest structures in the Kathmandu Durbar Square. Architecturally, Kavīndrapura has elements of both a temple and sataḥ . The three-tiered rectangular structure has traditional tiles laid on its roofs with three finials on top. The west

monument_description: Patako Ṭola is home to many Newar Hindu families such as Śreṣṭha.s, Amātyas, Rājabhaṇḍārīs, Rājopādhyāya and Brahmins. As such, there are few Hindu temples in the Ṭola. image_caption: Mahālakṣmī Mandira, view from NW
monument_description: This temple is located to the south of old Patan at Thasikhela Ṭola and has three tiers. The jhĩgaṭī tiles covering the roof of the temple were replaced by copper sheets in 1997. The temple's uppermost roof is gilded and surmounted by a pinnacle. An aniconic stone enshrined in the sanctum is worshipped as Mahālakṣmī and is framed by a metal toraṇa . Another aniconic stone (also housed in this temple) is worshipped as Viśvakarmā. The temple did not suffer any damages during the earthquakes of 193

monument_name: Jāhārkoṭa Devaldhārā (built between the 2nd half of the 14th cent. and the 15th cent.)
image_caption: Jāhārkoṭa Devaldhara, southern pillar, view from south
monument_description: A śikhara temple on top of a fountain from circa 14th-15th century. The monument is situated in the locality of Jāhārkoṭa, also known as Jārkoṭa, 3 km north of Narayan, the district headquarters. The śikhara temple is oriented to the west. Its door is preceeded by a pillared porch ( ardhamaṇḍapa ), an arrangement similar to that of the central temple of the nearby site of Pañcadevala ( DLK0070 ). image_caption: Yatkhā Bāhāḥ, view from E
monument_description: Yatkhā Bāhāḥ, also known as Bhāṣkarakīrti Vihāra, is located in a rectangular residential courtyard belonging to families of the Tāmrākāra caste group. The courtyard contains a large Caitya [1] in its center, and two smaller Caityas and a Lokeśvara Shrine. The dyaḥchẽ’s main door is flanked by a pair of lions, and statues of Śāriputra (right) and Maudgalyāyana (left). image_caption: Kinū Bahī, Navagraha Maṇḍala, view from S
monument_description: Kinū Bahī, also known as Lokakīrti Mahavihāra, is located in Iti Ṭola to the south of Mahapala Square. The earliest historical evidence of the bahī can be found in an inscription dated NS 538 (1418 CE). A carved door leads visitors through to the stone-paved courtyard of the bahī . A statue of Mahākāla is attached to the northern wall of the main entrance, and the courtyard contains a dharmadhātujinālaya caitya and a dharmadhātu ma ṇḍ ala . The bahī was reconstructed in 2012. The base of the śikhara is a twenty-sided shape known as viṃśatikoṇa (lit. twenty angles). A gilt copper pinnacle and trident are installed on the top of the śikhara. Positioned to face the north, Kālabhairava grasps a sword ( kha ḍ ga ), a trident ( trisūla ), and a skull cup ( kapālapātra ) with his three right hands, while his three left hands carry a shield, severed heads ( mu ṇḍ amālā ), and exhibit the bindu-mudrā , characterized by his palm facing outward

image_caption: Amṛta Hiti, view from SE
monument_description: Amṛta Hiti, locally known as Pvaḥ Hiti, was constructed during the medieval period for the Poḍhe community residing in Imūkhel, Lalitpur. The hiti has two water conduits, facing east. Generally, statues of Bhagīratha are installed under the water spout(s) of a hiti , however, this is not the case in Amṛta Hiti. Located at the Cvaylāchi Ṭola of Thecva, Bālakumārī Mandira is a rectangular structure with three tiers of copper roofs crowned with a gilded finial under the protection of a gilded parasol. image_caption: Ādikālī Mandira, view from E
monument_description: Adikālī Temple is an important Śākta pīṭha located in Baniyā̃ Village, Naikap (Nayā̃ Bhañjyāṅ), within Chandragiri Municipality. The shrine is situated on the highest rocky outcrop of the area, incorporating an ancient natural cave, a setting that confers both natural sanctity and historical significance on the site. The pīṭha was established directly upon this rock, and the temple structure was subsequently constructed atop it. To the south of the temple lies the Newar settlement known as Banth

image_caption: Umāmaheśvara Mandira at Cāka Bāhāḥ, full view of the temple from NW
monument_description: The Umāmaheśvara temple is located in Cāka Bāhāḥ Ṭola and stands on a three-tier plinth. It has a composite roof type which combines a tiered roof and a dome. Before 1934 earthquake, the temple had three tiers and was constructed in the style of a pagoda. However, it was levelled by the earthquake and reconstructed in this mixed style. This temple is located approximately 8 m east of the Nārāyaṇa Temple ( LAL2740 ), and together they form a complex with streets passing by to the west and south.

image_caption: Oku Bāhāḥ, courtyard, view from SW
monument_description: Oku Bāhāḥ (Śivadevasaṃskārita Rudravarṇa Mahāvihāra) is one of the oldest and most well-preserved bāhāḥs of Patan. 'Oku' is derived from 'õkuli', an ancient Newari word denoting a southeastern direction. Oku Bāhāḥ has 29 branches, the greatest number in the Valley and has the second largest Saṅgha ( Locke 1985, 90). image_caption: Calākhu Dabū, view from W
monument_description: The Calākhu Dabū is one of the five historic elevated platforms ( dabū s) constructed at separate locations of Sankhu. The Nārāyaṇa Mandira ( SKH0154 ) and Calākhu Phalcā ( SKH0153 ) stand south and east of this historic Dabū, respectively. The Dabū is also known as “Devī Dabū”, as the annual performance of the Devī Pyākhaṃ (lit. dance of goddess), a classical dance taken out from the Calākhu Ṭola on the 8th of the bright fortnight of Bhādra, is first exhibited here on this Dabū. image_caption: Calākhu Phalcā, gaḥjhyāḥ s, view from SW
monument_description: Standing east of the Calākhu Dabū ( SKH0152 ), this Phalcā has a corrugated tin roof, which diminishes its beauty. As the Phalcā does not have a specific name, it is referred to as 'Calākhu Phalcā' for this documentation based on its location in that Ṭola. There are no historical sources to date the initial construction of the Phalcā. A few thāṃ s (pillars) and two metha s (capitals) from its former structure survive at present. image_caption: Ajayamerukoṭa Fortress, structure DDL0180-007, view from NW
monument_description: The fortress is located on a rocky spur oriented north-west/south-east, at a few kilometres north-west of Amargadhi, the district headquarter. The site comprises a dozen of structures, including fortification walls and buildings of various shapes. Access to the site is made from the north-east. The entrance is controlled by a quadrangular tower. monument_description: The śikhara- style Santāneśvara Mahādeva temple is located in Vaṃghaḥ Ṭola, popularly known as Indrachowk. The stone śivali ṅ ga enshrined in the sanctum is worshipped as Santāneśvara Mahādeva. There are no historical records available to date the initial construction of the temple. image_caption: Agnimaṭha, back side, view from SE (September 2019)
monument_description: Agnimaṭha, also known as Agnisālā, is located in Thabu and is about 100 meters north of Kumārīpāṭī ( LAL4301 ). A road running south from Jena Bāhāḥ ( LAL0260 ) leads to this maṭha . A tilted Varūṇa tree obstructs the road that runs between the two monuments. A fire ritual ( homa ) is performed every day in the Agnimaṭha, as is Śuklayajurvedīya tradition. The fire for the ritual is produced by rubbing two pieces of wood together, and it is kept aflame for a long time. The Valley is home to three

image_caption: Śvetabhairava Mandira, view from E
monument_description: The three-storey Bhairava temple is based on a square plan measuring 257 cm on each side, and is based on a stone plinth that is 30 cm high and measures a length of 365 cm on each side. The temple has openings on all four sides, the main entrance is at the south. It is clad with repousse brass sheets and guarded by stone lions on either side.
